title: Supergenes Help Fish Evolve Quickly
summary: Scientists found special parts of DNA called 'supergenes' that help fish evolve faster. In Lake Malawi in Africa, over 800 types of cichlid fish have developed from one ancestor in a short time. These fish live in the same lake but have different jobs: some eat algae, some hunt, and others sift sand. The supergenes are like toolkits that keep useful traits together, such as good vision or hearing, so fish can adapt to places like deep water or sandy areas. Researchers from Cambridge and Antwerp studied the fish's DNA and discovered flipped DNA segments that stop genes from mixing during reproduction. This helps fish keep winning combinations of traits, making evolution quicker and allowing new species to form even when they live close together.

background_read: Evolution is how animals and plants change over many years to survive better. DNA is like a recipe book that tells living things how to grow and what traits to have. In Lake Malawi, cichlid fish show fast evolution because they adapted to different parts of the lake, like deep water or sandy areas, by using supergenes to keep helpful traits together.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from Cambridge and Antwerp and cichlid fish
WHAT: Found supergenes that speed up evolution by preserving useful traits
WHERE: Lake Malawi in East Africa
WHY: To understand how so many fish species evolved quickly in one lake

perspectives.description: Researchers found flipped DNA segments that act as supergenes to help fish adapt faster.
